summary: McKenzie argues forcefully that software developers who want to make money must limit their trial versions, rejecting the popular notion that donation-based models work. He cites Colin Messitt's controlled experiment showing limited versions outsold unlimited ones five to one, and Movable Type's average donation of 38 cents as evidence. He then explains his own approach with Bingo Card Creator, where he allows enough functionality to demonstrate value but not enough to complete the actual task (teaching a class). The post concludes that the right limitation strategy depends on deep knowledge of your target market and how they use your software.
key_points: A controlled experiment showed feature-limited shareware outsold unlimited donationware five to one, costing the developer $17,000 in lost sales from the unlimited group
Donation-based models produce negligible revenue — Movable Type's average donation was 38 cents despite powering thousands of blogs
The best trial limitation renders software 'attractive but very close to useless' for the actual job the customer needs done
Time-limited trials work best when the software becomes indispensable through daily use and accumulated configuration investment
Choosing the right limitation strategy requires deep understanding of how your specific target market actually uses the product
money_quotes: If you intend to make money by selling software, you need to limit your trial version. The only question is how.
The limited version outsold the unlimited one. Five to one. Colin calculates that the experiment cost him $17,000 in sales versus having 100% of the installations be limited.
Of all the people who thought Movable Type was worth paying real honest-to-God money for, the average donation was… 38 cents.

The reason I can limit my software like this is because I know enough about my target market to know what feature I can disable which will keep the software attractive but render it very close to useless.
If you're the kind of person who really needs Direct Access, after you've used it for a week you can't. Live. Without. It.
When you run into that 30 day limit and the convenience you have been relying on for 3+ weeks just shuts down, you whip out the credit card.
key_insight: Successful software trial design requires limiting the product just enough that users can verify its value but cannot complete their actual task without paying — and choosing the right limitation requires deep knowledge of how customers use the product.
